questions.stem: A nurse is caring for a client receiving IV heparin for DVT. The client's aPTT is 150 seconds (therapeutic 60-100 s). Which action is the priority?

options.text: Increase the heparin infusion rate by 100 units/hr.
Stop the heparin infusion and notify the provider.
Administer protamine sulfate immediately without awaiting orders.
Recheck the aPTT in 1 hour and document.

questions.rationale: An aPTT of 150 s is supratherapeutic (>2× upper limit). The infusion must be stopped and the provider notified before any dose adjustment. Protamine is the antidote but requires a provider order.
questions.takeaway: Supratherapeutic aPTT → hold heparin first, then follow protocol.

questions.stem: A client with type 1 diabetes presents with blood glucose 42 mg/dL, diaphoresis, and confusion. The client is unable to swallow safely. What is the nurse's first action?

options.text: Give 4 oz of orange juice by mouth.
Administer 1 mg glucagon IM.
Start a 5% dextrose IV infusion at 125 mL/hr.
Recheck capillary glucose in 15 minutes.

questions.rationale: Because the client cannot swallow safely, oral carbohydrate is contraindicated (aspiration risk). Glucagon IM raises glucose by stimulating hepatic glycogenolysis. IV dextrose is also correct but typically requires a provider order and IV access confirmation; glucagon is the fastest nurse-administered option when IV access is uncertain.
questions.takeaway: Hypoglycemia + unsafe swallow → glucagon IM, not oral glucose.

questions.stem: The nurse is preparing to delegate tasks to a UAP. Which task is appropriate to delegate?

options.text: Assess a postoperative client's incision for signs of infection.
Obtain a routine blood pressure on a stable client.
Teach a diabetic client foot-care techniques.
Interpret telemetry rhythm strips.

questions.rationale: UAPs may perform routine vital signs on stable clients. Assessment, teaching, and clinical interpretation require RN licensure and may not be delegated.
questions.takeaway: Delegate measurement of stable vitals; never delegate assessment, teaching, or interpretation.

questions.stem: A client on 4 L/min nasal cannula O₂ has SpO₂ 88%, RR 32, and accessory muscle use. ABGs: pH 7.28, PaCO₂ 58 mmHg, PaO₂ 54 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 26 mEq/L. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Increase nasal cannula flow to 6 L/min.
Apply a non-rebreather mask at 15 L/min.
Prepare for non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (NIPPV).
Obtain a stat chest X-ray and reassess.

questions.rationale: The ABG shows acute hypercapnic respiratory failure (pH 7.28, PaCO₂ 58) on supplemental O₂. NIPPV (BiPAP) addresses both oxygenation and ventilation and can avert intubation. Increasing flow alone does not correct hypoventilation. Imaging does not treat the acuity.
questions.takeaway: Hypercapnic failure + acidosis → NIPPV before escalating to intubation.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Oxygenation
visualRationale.caption: Hypercapnic failure + acidosis → NIPPV before escalating to intubation.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hypercapnic failure + acidosis → NIPPV before escalating to intubation.

questions.stem: The nurse is assigned to care for a client with active pulmonary tuberculosis. Which PPE is required when entering the room?

options.text: Surgical mask and gloves.
N95 respirator, gown, and gloves.
N95 respirator only.
Face shield, surgical mask, gown, and gloves.

questions.rationale: Active pulmonary TB requires airborne precautions (N95 or higher respirator) plus contact precautions (gown and gloves) when direct patient contact is anticipated.
questions.takeaway: Active TB → N95 + gown + gloves; negative-pressure room required.

questions.stem: A postoperative cardiac surgery patient has the following hemodynamic values: BP 82/54 mmHg, HR 118 bpm, CVP 2 mmHg, PCWP 4 mmHg, CO 2.8 L/min, SVR 2,100 dynes·s/cm⁵. Which category of shock is most consistent with these findings?

options.text: Distributive (septic) shock.
Obstructive shock.
Cardiogenic shock.
Hypovolemic shock.

questions.rationale: Low BP, tachycardia, low CVP (2), low PCWP (4), low CO, and markedly elevated SVR (compensatory vasoconstriction) indicate hypovolemic shock — the body is volume-depleted and the heart is pumping against maximum vasoconstriction. Cardiogenic shock has high PCWP; distributive has low SVR.

questions.stem: A patient in the CVICU develops new ST elevation in leads II, III, aVF, HR 44 bpm, BP 88/60 mmHg, and JVD. What is the most likely diagnosis and immediate priority?

options.text: Anterior STEMI — activate cath lab and give aspirin.
Inferior STEMI with RV involvement — give a 500 mL NS fluid bolus before nitrates.
Inferior STEMI — administer IV nitroglycerin for chest pain.
Pericarditis — start NSAIDs and monitor.

questions.rationale: ST elevation in II, III, aVF = inferior STEMI. Bradycardia, hypotension, and JVD suggest RV infarction. RV infarct patients are preload-dependent; nitrates are contraindicated (precipitate severe hypotension). Fluid resuscitation is the first priority, followed by cath-lab activation.
questions.takeaway: Inferior STEMI + hypotension + JVD → suspect RV infarct; no nitrates, give fluids.

visualRationale.title: Perfusion / Cardiac
visualRationale.caption: Inferior STEMI + hypotension + JVD → suspect RV infarct; no nitrates, give fluids.
visualRationale.conclusion: Inferior STEMI + hypotension + JVD → suspect RV infarct; no nitrates, give fluids.

questions.stem: A mechanically ventilated ARDS patient is on VC-AC, Vt 480 mL (6 mL/kg IBW), PEEP 12, FiO₂ 0.65, RR 22. Plateau pressure is 34 cmH₂O. What is the priority adjustment?

options.text: Increase Vt to 8 mL/kg to improve minute ventilation.
Reduce Vt by 1 mL/kg increments to target Pplat ≤30 cmH₂O.
Increase PEEP to 18 cmH₂O to open collapsed alveoli.
Switch to pressure control ventilation.

questions.rationale: ARDSnet protocol targets Pplat ≤30 cmH₂O to prevent volutrauma/barotrauma. A Pplat of 34 exceeds this threshold. Tidal volume should be decreased by 1 mL/kg increments (minimum 4 mL/kg IBW) until Pplat ≤30.
questions.takeaway: ARDS: target Vt 6 mL/kg IBW, Pplat ≤30 cmH₂O — reduce Vt if Pplat elevated.

visualRationale.title: Respiratory / Ventilator Management
visualRationale.caption: ARDS: target Vt 6 mL/kg IBW, Pplat ≤30 cmH₂O — reduce Vt if Pplat elevated.
visualRationale.conclusion: ARDS: target Vt 6 mL/kg IBW, Pplat ≤30 cmH₂O — reduce Vt if Pplat elevated.

questions.stem: A TBI patient has ICP 28 mmHg, MAP 80 mmHg, and CPP 52 mmHg. Glasgow Coma Scale is 7. Head is midline, HOB 30°. Which intervention should the nurse implement first?

options.text: Administer 3% NaCl 250 mL IV over 30 minutes.
Hyperventilate to PaCO₂ of 28 mmHg prophylactically.
Elevate HOB to 45° to improve venous drainage.
Perform endotracheal suctioning to clear secretions.

questions.rationale: CPP = MAP − ICP = 52 mmHg (target ≥60–70 mmHg), so both ICP reduction and MAP support are needed. Hypertonic saline (3% NaCl) reduces ICP via osmotic effect and is the priority. HOB at 30° is already set. Prophylactic hyperventilation causes harmful cerebral vasoconstriction and is not recommended. Suctioning transiently raises ICP.
questions.takeaway: Low CPP (<60) + elevated ICP → hypertonic saline; avoid prophylactic hyperventilation.

visualRationale.title: Neurology / ICP Management
visualRationale.caption: Low CPP (<60) + elevated ICP → hypertonic saline; avoid prophylactic hyperventilation.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low CPP (<60) + elevated ICP → hypertonic saline; avoid prophylactic hyperventilation.

questions.stem: A critical care patient develops oliguric acute kidney injury (AKI). Labs: Na 138, K 6.4 mEq/L, pH 7.22, HCO₃⁻ 14, BUN 88, Cr 4.2. ECG shows peaked T-waves. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Administer sodium polystyrene sulfonate (Kayexalate) per rectum.
Administer IV calcium gluconate immediately.
Initiate continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT).
Give sodium bicarbonate 50 mEq IV to treat acidosis.

questions.rationale: Peaked T-waves on ECG indicate cardiac membrane instability from hyperkalemia (K 6.4). IV calcium gluconate is the immediate priority to stabilize the cardiac membrane — it acts within minutes. Other therapies (kayexalate, bicarb, CRRT) lower potassium but take longer and do not immediately protect the heart.
questions.takeaway: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes → IV calcium gluconate first to stabilize cardiac membrane.

visualRationale.title: Renal / Fluid-Electrolyte
visualRationale.caption: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes → IV calcium gluconate first to stabilize cardiac membrane.
visualRationale.conclusion: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes → IV calcium gluconate first to stabilize cardiac membrane.
